behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned.
behaviors.justification: This is a logic-flow test where the failure is 'the code analyzes files it shouldn't.' A Unit-level test mocks the GitHub API call that fetches changed files (returning a specific set) and mocks the linter subprocess invocation. The test then asserts the linter was called with EXACTLY those changed files. A mock cannot miss this failure: if the code calls the linter with all repo files instead of just changed ones, the assertion on the linter invocation arguments will fail. No subprocess or I/O real behavior is required to catch this logic bug.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API to return a specific set of changed file paths (e.g., ['src/main.py', 'tests/test_main.py']). Mock the linter subprocess call. Run the pipeline. Assert that the linter subprocess was invoked with EXACTLY those file paths, no more, no fewer. Verify that files outside the changed set were NOT analyzed by confirming the mock was not called with those paths.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment.
behaviors.justification: This tests data formatting and grouping logic, not I/O or process behavior. The failure is 'the output comment doesn't group findings by severity correctly.' Mocking the linter to return mixed-severity findings and asserting the output comment text is properly structured (Errors section, then Warnings, then Info) will catch any grouping bug. A mock cannot miss this failure: if findings are not grouped or severities are mixed, the text assertion fails. No external infrastructure needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the linter to return a list of findings with mixed severities (e.g., [error1, warning1, error2, info1]). Run the comment-formatting logic. Assert the output comment text contains findings grouped by severity in order: Errors, Warnings, Info. Verify each group is properly delimited and findings within a group are listed in expected order.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout.
behaviors.justification: This is a performance/scalability test where the real failure is 'the linter + pipeline takes too long on a realistic workload.' A Unit-level mock (instant linter) hides the failure entirely because it doesn't measure real execution time. An Integration-level mock with simulated timing risks missing bottlenecks if assumptions are wrong. You MUST run a real linter subprocess on 200 real test files and measure wall-clock time. This requires System-level infrastructure: actual subprocess execution, real filesystem I/O, and timing measurements. Only then can you catch true performance regressions or missing parallelization.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test directory with 200 files of realistic variety (Python, JSON, YAML, mixed sizes) such that the real linter takes non-trivial time on each file. Run the actual linter pipeline using the real (or realistic) linter subprocess on all 200 files. Measure total wall-clock time from start to finish. Assert that completion time is less than a reasonable threshold (e.g., 5 minutes, leaving margin for GitHub Actions overhead). If linter is slow or code has inefficiencies (no parallelization, repeated re-scanning), the measured time will exceed the threshold and fail the test.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted.
behaviors.justification: This tests subprocess failure handling. The real failure is 'when the linter crashes on one file, the entire pipeline stops instead of continuing.' A Unit-level test that mocks in-process exceptions may not be representative because real subprocess failures manifest differently: exit codes, signals, timeouts. The code likely has specific logic for subprocess errors (check return codes, handle CalledProcessError) that differs from exception handling. An Integration-level test mocks the subprocess call to fail on a specific file, verifies the pipeline catches the error and continues, and confirms results are posted for non-failing files. This level is sufficient to catch the real failure without needing an actual crashing linter (System).
behaviors.test_description: Mock the linter subprocess to fail when processing file 'malformed.py' (e.g., return exit code 1 or raise subprocess.CalledProcessError). Provide 3 files to analyze: ['good1.py', 'malformed.py', 'good2.py']. Run the pipeline. Assert that: (1) the pipeline continues after the malformed.py failure, (2) findings from good1.py and good2.py are successfully captured, (3) the final PR comment is posted with findings from the successful files and an error note about the malformed file.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output.
behaviors.justification: This is pure data deduplication logic with no I/O or external behavior. The failure is 'duplicate findings appear in output when multiple rules flag the same line.' Mocking the linter to return duplicate findings and asserting the output has no duplicates will catch the failure. A mock cannot miss this: if the code fails to deduplicate, the output will contain duplicates and the assertion fails. No subprocess, filesystem, or network behavior needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the linter to return findings where the same line of code is flagged by multiple overlapping rules (e.g., rule A and rule B both flag line 10 in file.py with identical messages). Run the deduplication logic. Assert that the output findings contain each unique (file, line, message) tuple exactly once. Test multiple scenarios: exact duplicates, same line with different messages (should NOT deduplicate), and findings on different lines.
